title: Function templates
successMessage: One template, three types — the compiler generated three separate functions. myMax("z", "a") works because string has operator>. This is zero-cost generics — no boxing, no virtual dispatch.

title: Class templates
successMessage: Stack<int> and Stack<string> — one template, two specialisations. The stack pops in LIFO order — awesome, is, C++. This is exactly how std::stack is implemented.

title: Multiple template parameters
successMessage: Two type parameters — K and V. makePair uses auto to deduce the return type. This is exactly std::pair from <utility>, and std::map uses pairs for its key-value storage.

title: Template specialization
successMessage: Specializations override the general template for specific types. The general template uses to_string; the bool specialization uses true/false text. This is how type-specific behaviour is injected.

title: Build a generic container
instruction:
No starter code.

Write `template <typename T> class BoundedQueue`:
- Fixed max capacity (constructor parameter)
- `enqueue(T val)` — add to back, throw `overflow_error` if full
- `dequeue()` — remove and return front, throw `underflow_error` if empty
- `peek() const` — view front
- `size() const` / `isEmpty() const` / `isFull() const`

Test with `BoundedQueue<int>` and `BoundedQueue<string>`.
hint: Use vector<T> internally. Keep a deque-like structure or use front index + back index.

codeChecks.message: Test with both BoundedQueue<int> and BoundedQueue<string>.

successMessage: A generic bounded queue — works with any type. Templates with zero runtime overhead. This pattern is used in thread-safe work queues, message buffers, and event systems. Chapter 15 done!
